topic: Practising Formal Language and Noun Endings (-ance/-ence)
title: Making Sentences More Formal: Practice with -ance and -ence
simple_explanation: This page is like a practice playground for turning 'describing words' into more 'official-sounding words' by adding '-ance' or '-ence'. You'll be making your sentences sound super smart and formal, just like a grown-up writer!
detailed_explanation: Rewriting sentences to use these noun forms can make your arguments sound more sophisticated and academic. It shows you can use language very precisely. Pay attention to which words use '-ance' and which use '-ence' – it can be tricky, but regular practice with 'look, cover, write, check' will help you remember the spellings.

examples_expanded.text: Instead of 'The palace was magnificent and I was impressed,' you can say 'I was impressed by the **magnificence** of the palace.' See how 'magnificence' (a noun) makes the sentence more formal?
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of practising formal language and noun endings (-ance/-ence) in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: If 'my teacher appeared' and 'this surprised me,' you can combine that to say 'The **appearance** of my teacher surprised me.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of practising formal language and noun endings (-ance/-ence) in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: You can talk about 'the **independence** of the speakers' instead of saying 'the speakers were independent'.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of practising formal language and noun endings (-ance/-ence) in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'magnificence' in a sentence. Remember it means: the quality of being magnificent; grandeur
Match the word 'appearance' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Practising Formal Language and Noun Endings (-ance/-ence).
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'magnificence' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Practice converting adjectives to nouns using '-ance' and '-ence' for more formal writing.
Identify words with '-ance' and '-ence' endings in context.
Use spelling strategies like 'look, cover, write, check' to master these challenging words.

vocabulary.definition: the quality of being magnificent; grandeur
the way that someone or something looks
the quality or state of being urgent; a need for immediate action
the ability to learn and apply knowledge and skills
the state of being innocent of a crime or wrongdoing
the state or fact of existing, occurring, or being present
the state or fact of being of great significance or value
the capacity to have an effect on the character, development, or behaviour of someone or something
the state of being independent; freedom from outside control or support
a state in which opposing forces or influences are equal
graceful and stylish in appearance or manner
plain or obvious; clearly seen or understood
differ in size, amount, degree, or nature from something else of the same general class
a person or group that provides assistance and support in an ongoing effort, activity, or struggle
prevent (a process or activity) from continuing or being carried out properly
tell someone something positively to remove any doubt
